title: Percentile Calculator for Data Points 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate what percentile a value falls in within your dataset. Find quartiles and statistical rankings with detailed step-by-step results.
intro: A **[percentile](/percentile-height-weight-baby-who)** shows what percentage of data is less than or equal to a given value. If you're at the 80th percentile, you rank higher than 80% of the dataset. This calculator determines the percentile of any value within your data and includes quartiles Q1, Q2, and Q3.
keyTakeaway: If your value ranks higher than **80 out of 100 data points**, you're at the **80th percentile** (better than 80% of the group).
useCases: Determine what percentile an exam score falls in.
Evaluate a measurement against a reference group.

faq.a: A percentile shows what **percentage of the data is less than or equal to** a specific value. If you're at the 80th percentile, you rank higher than 80% of the group.
Sort your data in ascending order, count how many values are ≤ your target value, divide by the total count, and multiply by 100: **Percentile = (count ≤ value / total) × 100**.
Quartiles divide data into four equal parts: **Q1** = 25th percentile, **Q2** = 50th percentile (median), **Q3** = 75th percentile. They show how data is distributed.

faq.q: Is a high percentile good or bad?
faq.a: It depends on context. For test scores and grades: high is good. For wait times or error rates: high is bad. Always consider what's being measured.

faq.q: How many data points do I need?
faq.a: At least 3–5 points for basic calculations, but results are more reliable with 20+ data points. With 30+ values, percentiles become statistically stable.
The IQR = Q3 − Q1. It measures the spread of the middle 50% of your data and identifies outliers: values below Q1 − 1.5×IQR or above Q3 + 1.5×IQR.

faq.q: What's the difference between percentile and percentage?
faq.a: Percentage is any proportion (e.g., 25% of 100). Percentile refers to your **relative ranking** within a sorted group. The 80th percentile means you rank at position 80%.

faq.q: How do I interpret my percentile rank?
faq.a: Your percentile shows where you stand relative to others. The 75th percentile means you're in the top 25%, performing better than 75% of people or values in the group.

explanation:
## What is a percentile?

A **percentile** indicates what percentage of a distribution is **less than or equal to** a given value. If your height falls at the **80th percentile**, you're taller than 80% of the sample. It's a relative ranking measure, widely used in healthcare, education (standardized test scores), psychometrics, and descriptive statistics.

## Percentiles and quartiles at a glance

| Percentile | Quartile | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| P25 | Q1 | 25% below, 75% above |
| P50 | Q2 (median) | Exact midpoint |
| P75 | Q3 | 75% below, 25% above |
| P90 | — | Top 10% |
| P99 | — | Top 1% |

## How to calculate percentiles

Using linear interpolation:
1. Sort all your data in ascending order.
2. For a target value V, count how many values are ≤ V to get **k**.
3. Percentile = (k / n) × 100, where n is the total number of values.

Some methods use (k − 0.5)/n to reduce bias in small samples.

## When to use percentiles and common mistakes

- **Don't confuse percentile with percentage**: A 90th percentile doesn't mean 90 points earned.
- **Small samples** (< 20 values) produce unreliable percentiles.
- **The 50th percentile equals the median**, not the mean.
- For deeper analysis, try [linear regression](/linear-regression-simple) with your data.

example.title: Real-world example: Exam score
steps: **Calculation**: 7 values are less than or equal to 75 (all up to 70), plus interpolation.
**Percentile**: ~70. You rank higher than 70% of the class.
example.result: Your score of 75 places you at the 70th percentile: better than 70% of the class, above the median.
